summary: I was born in Seattle, Washington, on September 18, 1952. My family settled in the north part of Seattle, specifically in Shoreline, where I grew up. Seattle was the place where I started my career, working with the Port of Seattle and later joining the ILWU. It’s been home to me since birth and a big part of my life story.

My parents both came from Canada and moved to the U.S. in 1951 for better work opportunities. They were both in the Canadian Air Force before they were discharged and decided to settle down in the Seattle area. My older siblings were born in Canada but later became U.S. citizens. Canada is where my family roots are, and it shaped my parents' early lives and careers.

Both my parents served in the Canadian Air Force before moving to the United States. My dad was an electronic technician while in the military. After they were discharged, they sought better work opportunities down here in Seattle. Their military background was a big part of their identity, even though they eventually transitioned to civilian life in the U.S.

I grew up in Shoreline, Washington, which is in north Seattle. It was a very white, suburban community in the 1950s and 60s, kind of a Leave-It-to-Beaver type of environment. I attended Shoreline High School and later Shoreline Community College. Growing up there was a good experience with a close-knit family and neighborhood.

I became involved with the International Longshore and Warehouse Union when women were first allowed to become longshoremen in 1980. Before that, I worked closely with longshoremen at the Port of Seattle, preparing paperwork and coordinating with Customs. The ILWU was a big part of my career and identity, and I was proud to be one of the first women to join this traditionally male union.

After finishing some college, I worked for Mitsui O.S.K. Lines for about four years. This job connected me directly with the Port of Seattle and its operations. It was through this work that I met people like Frank Clark, who encouraged me to apply for a position at the Port. Mitsui O.S.K. Lines was my first real step into the maritime industry.

When women were first allowed to become longshoremen in 1980, I went through an interview process that included a representative from the Pacific Maritime Association. The PMA guy didn’t say much during my interview, but it was clear they were part of the hiring process. The union representative asked if I thought I could do the job, and I promised to give it 110 percent. The PMA was an important organization in the background of my entry into longshore work.
